coredata.dc:title: Carbon nanodots functionalized with rhodamine and poly(ethylene glycol) for ratiometric sensing of Al ions in aqueous solution
coredata.dc:creator: Kim, Yujun
Jang, Geunseok
Lee, Taek Seung
coredata.dc:description: Carbon nanodots (CDs) functionalized with a rhodamine moiety and poly(ethylene glycol) (PEG) were used for selective fluorometric detection of Al ions. When the Al ion was exposed to the modified CDs, the lactam ring of the rhodamine moiety opened and, subsequently, was ready to absorb the emission of CDs, leading to a Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET)-based sensing protocol in aqueous media. A selective response to Al ion was observed in an aqueous solution, which was attributed to the ability of the rhodamine moiety to interact with the Al ion, but not with other metal ions. PEG played a role in improving the water-solubility of rhodamine-appended CDs that were intrinsically hydrophobic. The FRET-based mechanism provideed a ratiometric sensing that could remove possible interference via self-calibration of two emission bands.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodots sensors
Rhodamine
Al ion
FRET
PEGylation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CDs with R6G and PEG (R6G-PEG@CDs)

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Financial support from the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by Korean government through the Basic Science Research Program (2015R1A2A2A05001134) is gratefully acknowledged.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthetic routes to R6G@CDs and R6G-PEG@CDs.
Schematic illustration for FRET-based Al ion-detection mechanism of R6G-PEG@CDs.

(a) Changes in fluorescence spectra and (b) relationship between the changes in fluorescence (I555/I410) of R6G-PEG@CDs with various concentrations of Al ion in aqueous solution. Arrow direction: [Al3+]=0; 1.0×10−6; 5.0×10−6; 1.0×10−5; 5.0×10−5; 1.0×10−4; 5.0×10−4; 1.0×10−3; 5.0×10−3; 1.0×10−2 M. Inset shows the linear response at low concentrations of Al ions. Excitation wavelength λex =350nm.

(a) Changes in fluorescence spectra and (b) relative fluorescence intensity (I555/I410) of R6G-PEG@CDs in aqueous solution upon exposure to various metal ions. [Metal ions]=1.0×10−3 M. Excitation wavelength λex =350nm.

head.ce:title: Carbon nanodots functionalized with rhodamine and poly(ethylene glycol) for ratiometric sensing of Al ions in aqueous solution

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Organic and Optoelectronic Materials Laboratory, Department of Organic Materials Engineering, Chungnam National University, Daejeon 34134, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon nanodots (CDs) functionalized with a rhodamine moiety and poly(ethylene glycol) (PEG) were used for selective fluorometric detection of Al ions. When the Al ion was exposed to the modified CDs, the lactam ring of the rhodamine moiety opened and, subsequently, was ready to absorb the emission of CDs, leading to a Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET)-based sensing protocol in aqueous media. A selective response to Al ion was observed in an aqueous solution, which was attributed to the ability of the rhodamine moiety to interact with the Al ion, but not with other metal ions. PEG played a role in improving the water-solubility of rhodamine-appended CDs that were intrinsically hydrophobic. The FRET-based mechanism provideed a ratiometric sensing that could remove possible interference via self-calibration of two emission bands.

ce:section.ce:para: Al is the most abundant metal on earth and is used extensively in modern daily life and industrial fields [1]. Excessive amounts of Al ions make water acidic, creating a harsh environment for aquatic lives [2] and harming human health, and inducing Parkinson disease [3] and Alzheimer disease [4]. The World Health Organization listed Al ion as a food pollution source and indicated that Al ion in drinking water should be limited to the concentration of 200μg/L (7.41μM) [5]. Consequently, it becomes important to develop an easy Al ion-sensing method for environmental monitoring and biological assays. Fluorescence sensing techniques have received considerable attention because of their excellent properties of high sensitivity, selectivity, cost-efficiency, and real-time monitoring [6]. Various fluorescence-based methods have been developed for Al ion-detection using organic materials [7–11]. However, these techniques have the disadvantage of poor photostability and most only work well in organic solvents [12]. In addition, these methods mainly based on single wavelength-intensity modulation type sensors (turn-on or turn-off sensors) and, thus, lack of self-calibration ability; therefore environmental noise, including background interference is possible [13,14]. Ratiometric sensing is highly demanding because its intrinsic correction ability can effectively reduce external effects, compared with the turn-on or turn-off sensors affected by various factors including photobleaching, probe concentration, or light-source stability [15,16]. Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) based measurement of two emission intensities at different wavelengths, which involves nonradiative transfer of excitation energy from an excited donor to ground-state acceptor, has been widely adopted in ratiometric detection because using the ratio between two interconnected fluorescence signals as the detection index could effectively eliminate background interference [17].

Recently, carbon nanodots (CDs) have attracted considerable attention because of their low toxicity, high water solubility, chemical stability, photostability, cost-effectiveness, and biocompatibility [18–22]. Because of these excellent properties, CDs have been applied to versatile fields, such as bioimaging [23], chemosensing [24], temperature sensing [25], photocatalysis [26], and photothermal therapy [27]. CDs have been prepared by various techniques, such as arc-discharge, combustion, hydrothermal synthesis, microwave pyrolysis, laser ablation, and plasma treatments [28–33]. CDs possess many hydrophilic groups on their surface and thus can be easily dissolved in aqueous media without further surface modification. However, the exploration of CDs as possible fluorescent sensor for monitoring analytes needs surface modification to introduce other ligand or fluorophores, which, in turn, diminished their solubility in aqueous media. As a result, the introduction of a hydrophilic group is essential for developing versatile CDs-based sensors in aqueous solution [34,35].

To attain a successful FRET-based ratiometric sensor protocol, we introduced two fluorophores in a system that consisted of CDs as the energy donor and a rhodamine moiety as the acceptor. Rhodamine derivatives with spirolactam rings are colorless and nonfluorescent, whereas the open-ring form gives rise to the appearance of a pink color and strong emission upon exposure to metal ions [36,37]. Rhodamine derivatives are ideal platforms to construct an on-off system (color and fluorescence) and related research has also been carried out to develop such probes for various metal ions [38]. When the rhodamine fluorophore was incorporated on the surface of the CDs, the water-solubility of the modified CDs greatly decreased because of the hydrophobic nature of the rhodamine. Such hydrophobic CDs are ready to aggregate, which leads to unexpected nonspecific binding [39]. To overcome this shortcoming, poly(ethylene glycol) (PEG), a neutral water-soluble polymer, was incorporated on the surface of the CDs to reduce unfavorable aggregation (improve colloidal stability), which leads to enhanced water-solubility and to the elimination of nonspecific binding to targets (selectivity) [35,40]. In this study, we investigated CDs with PEG, as well as the rhodamine moiety, which was a highly efficient ratiometric FRET-based probe in aqueous solution. The changes in fluorescence signals at two different emission wavelengths were clarified for the detection of Al ions via a FRET-based mechanism.

All chemicals were obtained from commercial sources and used without further purification. Acrylic acid, dichloromethane, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) and ethanol was purchased from Samchun Pure Chemical (Seoul, Korea). 1,2-Ethylenediamine, rhodamine 6G (R6G), succinic acid, O-[(N-succinimidyl)succinyl-aminoethyl]-O′-methylpolyethylene glycol (Mw ∼750), N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), N,N'-dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCC), 4-(dimethylamino)pyridine (DMAP), and dialysis membranes (molecular weight cut-off [MWCO] 2000 and 13000) were purchased from Aldrich (St Louis, MO). Cd2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Al3+, Zn2+ with perchlorate counter ions, and mercury(II) chloride were purchased from Aldrich. Aqueous solutions of metal ions were prepared with deionized water.

1H NMR spectra were obtained on a Bruker DRX-300 spectrometer (Bruker, Billerica, MA) with tetramethylsilane as the internal standard (Korea Basic Science Institute). Infrared (IR) spectra were obtained on a Bruker FT-IR spectrometer. UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained on a Perkin-Elmer Lambda 35 spectrometer (Perkin-Elmer, Waltham, MA). Photoluminescence spectra were obtained on a Varian Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, Santa Clara, CA) equipped with a Xe flash lamp excitation source. Photographs were taken using a Canon Powershot A95 digital camera (Canon Melville, NY) under a hand-held UV lamp. Samples for transmission electron microscopy (TEM) were prepared by dropping the CDs solution onto a 300-mesh copper grid coated with a carbon film. TEM images were obtained on JEM-3010 (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan) operating at 200kV accelerating voltage, using the images acquired with an ORIUS-SC 600 CCD camera (Gatan, Inc. Warrendale, PA). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrum of the sample was measured on a MultiLab 2000 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo VG Scientific, Waltham, MA). Zeta potentials were measured by a Zetasizer Nano ZS90 apparatus (Malvern, Malvern, UK).

R6G-COOH (10mg, 0.018mmol) and CDs (10mg) were placed into a flask containing dry DMSO (10mL) under nitrogen. DMAP (200mg, 1.64mmol) and DCC (340mg, 1.64mmol) were added to the flask and stirred at room temperature for 24h. The mixture was extracted with dichloromethane and water to remove unreacted CDs. Finally, the organic phase was collected and dried in vacuum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs with R6G and PEG (R6G-PEG@CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: O-[(N-Succinimidyl)succinyl-aminoethyl]-O′-methylpolyethylene glycol (10mg) and R6G@CDs (10mg) were dissolved in DMSO (10mL). The reaction mixture was stirred at room temperature for 24h. Next, the reaction mixture was freeze-dried to remove DMSO. The crude product was purified by dialysis (using MWCO 2000) against pure water for 48h. Finally, the purified products were dried in a freeze-drying oven.

For the construction of the FRET-based Al ion detection system, we synthesized CDs according to the literature method (Scheme S1) [41]. The detailed preparation method is described in Supplementary Information. The as-prepared CDs showed UV–vis absorption at 300nm, attributed to π−π* transition of the sp2 hybridized structure of CDs (Fig. S1a). Generally, CDs show various fluorescence emissions that are highly dependent on the excitation wavelength. The fluorescence spectra of the CDs were investigated in an aqueous solution (1mg/mL) with different excitations. Upon excitation at 340nm, the CDs exhibited strong blue emission around 410nm. In addition, red-shifted emissions were observed at different excitations (Fig. S1b). The chemical structure of CDs was investigated using FT-IR and XPS. The FT-IR spectrum revealed the presence of amino groups in the CDs in which CN and NH characteristic bands were observed at 1288cm−1 and 3408cm−1, respectively (Fig. S2a). XPS spectrum showed that CDs were composed of C, O, and N, where N 1s of the CDs corresponded to NH (399.63eV), indicating that the CDs were successfully functionalized with the amino group (Fig. S2). The zeta-potential of the CDs was found to be positive (+41.3mV), mainly because of the presence of amino groups on their surface. The TEM image of the as-prepared CDs showed them to be well dispersed with an average diameter of about 20nm (Fig. S3a).

To modify the surface of CDs, R6G-COOH, an R6G derivative, was synthesized via two-step reactions according to the literature (Scheme S1) [42]. The intermediate R6G-NH2 was synthesized by a reaction with R6G and 1,2-ethylenediamine with a yield of 65%. Then, R6G-COOH was obtained by a reaction between R6G-NH2 and succinic acid using a conventional EDC/NHS coupling reaction in DMF solution at room temperature. The chemical structures of R6G-NH2 and R6G-COOH were confirmed by [1] H NMR. R6G-COOH was introduced on the surface of CDs via amide formation reaction using DCC/DMAP to obtain R6G@CDs, which were CDs modified with a covalently-bound rhodamine derivative (Scheme 1 ). To improve the poor water-solubility of the R6G@CDs, hydrophilic linear PEG was used as a surface passivation moiety. The PEGylated CDs were synthesized by amidation of the amino group remaining on the surface of R6G@CDs with the aid of O-[(N-succinimidyl)succinyl-aminoethyl]-O′-methylpolyethylene glycol. After the PEGylation, zeta potential of R6G-PEG@CDs was changed to +1.23mV. FT-IR spectra of R6G-PEG@CDs shows intensified characteristic band of CO group (amide bond) at 1650cm−1 (Fig. S2a). TEM images indicated that the surface of R6G- PEG@CDs is covered with a brighter layer, which should be PEG derived from the amide coupling reaction with CDs (Fig. S3b). Thus, TEM images demonstrated the successful conjugation of PEG molecules on the surface of CDs. Ultimately, rhodamine and PEG derivatives were successfully introduced on the surface of CDs, inducing R6G-PEG@CDs. After the PEGylation of R6G@CDs, R6G-PEG@CDs exhibited much enhanced solubility and stability in water.

The rhodamine moiety in R6G-PEG@CDs was designed to interact with Al ions, in which the lactam ring of the rhodamine moiety would open upon exposure to Al ions and, in turn, the open-ring rhodamine serves as the energy acceptor to provide FRET. To obtain an efficient FRET, two requirements should be fulfilled: 1) the emission band of the donor should overlap the absorption of the acceptor; 2) the distance between two species should be less than 10nm [43,44]. In the absence of Al ions, the lactam ring-containing rhodamine does not absorb the emission of CDs because of minimal spectral overlap of the two species (CDs and spirolactam-rhodamine). As Al ions were added, the emission of CDs should have been absorbed by the open-ring rhodamine moiety located on the CD surface. As a result, the red emission of the open-ring rhodamine would be amplified according to AL ion concentrations (Scheme 2 ).

To match the donor emission and acceptor absorption, 350nm was selected as the excitation wavelength of CDs. The fluorescence emission of CDs matched well with the absorption of R6G around 526nm, expecting the emission of CDs to transfer to the rhodamine on the CDs (Fig. S4). To ensure spontaneous energy transfer from CDs to the open-ring rhodamine on the CDs, the fluorescence spectra of R6G-PEG@CDs were investigated at pH 4 with different excitation wavelengths (350nm and 526nm), in which the lactam ring was open at pH 4 (Fig. 1 ). The emission intensity of R6G-PEG@CDs at pH 4 under excitation at 350nm (corresponding to the absorption of the CDs) was two times higher than that under direct excitation at 526nm (corresponding to the absorption of rhodamine), implying that the energy transfer from CDs to rhodamine was spontaneous. Only blue emission of CDs was observed at pH 7 (416nm), in which the ring-opening of the lactam did not take place, which is indicative of a negligible FRET. The results clearly demonstrated that the FRET occurred from CDs to the rhodamine moiety in aqueous solution.

The changes in fluorescence intensity of R6G-PEG@CDs were measured upon addition of varying Al ion concentration. Initially, excitation of R6G-PEG@CDs at 350nm led to blue emission at 410nm without Al ion. The gradual decrease in emission at 410nm and the concomitant increase in a new emission band at 555nm for R6G-PEG@CDs was observed with increasing Al ion concentration (Fig. 2 a). The fluorescence of R6G-PEG@CDs changed instantaneously upon exposure to the Al ion, indicating that the emission energy of CDs was easily transferred to the open-ring rhodamine moiety. The intensity ratio of two emissions at 555nm and 410nm (I555/I410) increased with concentration range of Al ion from 0 to 10mM, with a linear range up to 5×10−5 M (Fig. 2b). The limit of detection (LOD) for R6G-PEG@CDs was determined as 1.8×10−5 M (3σ/slope, σ=standard derivation), in which the result was comparable to previous reports [10,45,46].

To elucidate the sensor performance of R6G-PEG@CDs, the fluorescence response of these compounds to other metal ions in aqueous solution was investigated. Fig. 3 a shows the changes in the fluorescence intensity of R6G-PEG@CDs in the presence of various metal ions in aqueous solution. Compared to changes in emissions by other metal ions, Al ions provided a noticeable increase in the emission at 555nm induced by FRET. For direct comparison of the ratiometric changes in two emissions, the ratio of the two emissions (I555/I410) with various metal ions including Al was demonstrated at the same metal concentration of 1.0×10−3 M. The results indicated that R6G-PEG@CDs exhibited high selectivity toward the Al ion compared with other metal ions in terms of the ratiometric sensing method. To investigate selectivity further, a comparative study was conducted in the presence of R6G-PEG@CDs with a mixture of Al ion and other metal ions. Fig. 4 shows that the fluorescence response of R6G-PEG@CDs toward the Al ion was not affected by the presence of other metal ions, which demonstrates that our sensor material could meet the selective requirements for environmental applications.

Rhodamine-functionalized, PEGylated CDs were synthesized via an amidation reaction for fluorogenic and ratiometric detection of Al ions in aqueous solution. The sensing CDs exhibited high selectivity toward Al ion over several other metal ions because of the rhodamine functionality. FRET was successfully accomplished because of the spectral overlap of the emission of CDs and the absorption of the rhodamine moiety, which was the basic mechanism of the sensing protocol, exhibiting high selectivity and detection limit. The CDs and rhodamine moiety served as energy donor and acceptor, respectively, where the PEG worked for improving low solubility of the rhodamine-functionalized CDs in aqueous solution. Our sensing system enabled an observation of double-channel fluorescence signal changes. We expect our probe to offer a new approach to FRET-based ratiometric systems for detecting metal ions in aqueous media, such as analysis in live cell media and environmental applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support from the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by Korean government through the Basic Science Research Program (2015R1A2A2A05001134) is gratefully acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.04.055.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

Intraneuronal aluminum accumulation in amyotrophic lateral sclerosis and Parkinsonism-dementia of Guam
Aluminum speciation in relation to aluminum bioavailability, metabolism and toxicity
Reference to Addendum to Vol. 2 in an Edited Book: Health Criteria and Other Supporting Information

A novel molecular probe sensing polynuclear hydrolyzed aluminum by chelation-enhanced fluorescence
Imine linked fluorescent chemosensor for Al3+ and resultant complex as a chemosensor for HSO4 − anion
Thiazole Schiff base turn-on fluorescent chemosensor for Al3+ ion
Highly fluorescent conjugated polyelectrolyte nanostructures: synthesis, self-assembly, and Al3+ Ion Sensing
A fluorescent and colorimetric sensor for Al3+ based on a dibenzo-18-crown-6 derivative
Highly selective ion probe for Al3+ based on Au (i)⋯ Au (i) interactions in a bis-alkynyl calix [4] arene Au (i) isocyanide scaffold
Reaction-based fluorescent probes for selective imaging of hydrogen sulfide in living cells
Reaction based fluorescent probes for hydrogen sulfide
Intercalating dye harnessed cationic conjugated polymer for real-time naked-eye recognition of double-stranded DNA in serum
Visual optical discrimination and detection of microbial pathogens based on diverse interactions of conjugated polyelectrolytes with cells
A FRET-based ratiometric sensor for mercury ions in water with multi-layered silica nanoparticles as the scaffold
Carbon dots with continuously tunable full-color emission and their application in ratiometric pH sensing
Functional surface engineering of C-dots for fluorescent biosensing and in vivo bioimaging
Carbon dots as nontoxic and high-performance fluorescence imaging agents
Carbon-dot-based nanosensors for the detection of intracellular redox state
In vivo NIR fluorescence imaging, biodistribution, and toxicology of photoluminescent carbon dots produced from carbon nanotubes and graphite
Electrochemiluminescence of a nano Ag–carbon nanodot composite and its application to detect sulfide ions
Photoluminescent organosilane-functionalized carbon dots as temperature probes
Water-soluble fluorescent carbon quantum dots and photocatalyst design
PEGylated carbon nanoparticles for efficient in vitro photothermal cancer therapy
Making graphene luminescent by oxygen plasma treatment
Electrophoretic analysis and purification of fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotube fragments
Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles derived from candle soot
One-step synthesis of amino-functionalized fluorescent carbon nanoparticles by hydrothermal carbonization of chitosan
Highly luminescent carbon nanodots by microwave-assisted pyrolysis
A tunable ratiometric pH sensor based on carbon nanodots for the quantitative measurement of the intracellular pH of whole cells
Nitrogen-doped, thiol-functionalized carbon dots for ultrasensitive Hg (II) detection
A long-wavelength fluorescent chemodosimeter selective for Cu(II) ion in water
A rhodamine-based fluorescent and colorimetric chemodosimeter for the rapid detection of Hg2+ ions in aqueous media
Fluorescent chemosensors based on spiroring-opening of xanthenes and related derivatives
Water-soluble conjugated polymers for imaging diagnosis, and therapy
A water-soluble conjugated polymer with pendant disulfide linkages to PEG chains: a highly efficient ratiometric probe with solubility-induced fluorescence conversion for thiol detection
A facile and versatile approach to biocompatible fluorescent polymers from polymerizable carbon nanodots
Synthesis of an amphiphilic copolymer bearing rhodamine moieties and its self-assembly into micelles as chemosensors for Fe3+ in aqueous solution
Studies of synthetic polymers by nonradiative energy transfer
Synthesis of conjugated, hyperbranched copolymers for tunable multicolor emissions in light-emitting diodes
A colorimetric and turn-on fluorescent chemosensor for Al (III) based on a chromone Schiff-base
A highly selective colorimetric and turn-on fluorescent chemosensor based on 1-(2-pyridylazo)-2-naphthol for the detection of aluminium(III) ions

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yujun Kim obtained his BSc in advanced organic materials and textile system engineering from Chungnam National University in 2014. Now he is studying in Organic and Optoelectronic Materials Laboratory at Chungnam National University. His current research interests are carbon-based nanohybrid sensor.

Geunseok Jang received his master degree from Chungnam National University in 2012. Now he is studying as a PhD student in organic and optoelectronic materials lab at Chungnam National University. His main fields are sensors and organic chemistry.

Taek Seung Lee received a BS in textile engineering from Seoul National University in 1988 where he obtained a PhD in fiber and polymer science in 1994. After post-doctoral researches at Korea Institute of Science and Technology and University of Massachusetts Lowell, he joined Chungnam National University as an assistant professor in 1997 and became a full professor in 2008. His research interest includes synthesis of functional organic materials and construction of hybrid nanomaterials, which have potential uses in OLED and chemical/biological sensors.
